solution: By LIATE choose \( u = [[c]]x \) (algebraic) and \( dv = \cos([[n]]x)\,dx \). Then \( \int_{0}^{\pi} [[c]]x\cos([[n]]x)\,dx = \left.\left(\tfrac{[[c]]x\sin([[n]]x)}{[[n]]} + \tfrac{[[c]]\cos([[n]]x)}{[[n]]^{2}}\right)\right|_{0}^{\pi} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the sign that comes from \( \cos([[n]]\pi) = -1 \) for odd \( [[n]] \).
This keeps the sign but omits the factor of 2 from the difference of endpoint cosines.
This uses the wrong power of \( [[n]] \) in the denominator (\( [[n]] \) instead of \( [[n]]^{2} \)).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Unlike the cosine version of this integral, here the BOUNDARY term survives and the remaining integral vanishes. Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi} [[c]]x\sin\left([[n]]x\right)\,dx \).
solution: By LIATE choose \( u=[[c]]x \), \( dv=\sin([[n]]x)\,dx \), so \( v=-\tfrac{\cos([[n]]x)}{[[n]]} \). The boundary term \( \left.-\tfrac{[[c]]x\cos([[n]]x)}{[[n]]}\right|_{0}^{\pi} = -\tfrac{[[c]]\pi\cos([[n]]\pi)}{[[n]]} \) is nonzero, while the remaining integral \( \int_0^\pi \tfrac{[[c]]\cos([[n]]x)}{[[n]]}\,dx \) integrates to \( \tfrac{[[c]]\sin([[n]]x)}{[[n]]^2} \), which vanishes at both \( 0 \) and \( \pi \). So the answer is entirely the boundary term: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the boundary term vanishes here the way it does for \( x\cos(nx) \).
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This drops the factor of \( \pi \) picked up from evaluating at the upper limit.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: By LIATE the algebraic factor is \( u \) and the exponential is \( dv \); the boundary term vanishes as \( x\to\infty \) because the exponential decays faster than \( x \) grows. Evaluate the improper integral \( \int_{0}^{\infty} [[c]]x\,e^{-[[k]]x}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=[[c]]x \), \( dv=e^{-[[k]]x}dx \), \( v=-\tfrac{1}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x} \), integration by parts gives \( \left.-\tfrac{[[c]]x}{[[k]]}e^{-[[k]]x}\right|_0^{\infty} + \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]}\displaystyle\int_0^\infty e^{-[[k]]x}dx \). The boundary term is \( 0 \) at both ends, and the remaining integral is \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]}\cdot\dfrac{1}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This stops after the first application of parts, reporting only the \( c/k \) coefficient without the second factor of \( 1/k \).
This inverts the exponent's role, multiplying by \( k \) instead of dividing.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: By LIATE the logarithm outranks the power of \( x \), so take \( u=\ln x \) and \( dv = x^{[[n]]}\,dx \). Evaluate \( \int_{1}^{[[m]]} x^{[[n]]}\ln x\,dx \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the \( \tfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}\ln x \) piece and forgets to subtract the \( \tfrac{x^{n+1}}{(n+1)^2} \) correction term.
This keeps only the correction-term structure but drops the leading power of \( [[m]] \).
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Even a single function like \( \ln x \) needs integration by parts: write \( \ln x = 1\cdot\ln x \) and take \( u=\ln x \), \( dv=dx \). Evaluate \( \int_{1}^{[[m]]} [[c]]\ln x\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=\ln x \), \( dv=dx \), \( v=x \): \( \int \ln x\,dx = x\ln x - x \). Evaluated from \( 1 \) to \( [[m]] \) (where \( 1\cdot\ln 1 - 1 = -1 \)) and scaled by \( [[c]] \), this gives \( [[c]]\left(([[m]]\ln [[m]] - [[m]]) - (-1)\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the \( -x \) term entirely, keeping only \( x\ln x \).
This forgets the lower-limit contribution of \( -1 \) from \( x\ln x - x \) at \( x=1 \).
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: By LIATE the inverse-trig function outranks the (implicit) algebraic factor, so take \( u=\arctan x \), \( dv=dx \). Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[b]]} [[c]]\arctan x\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=\arctan x \), \( dv=dx \), \( v=x \): \( \int \arctan x\,dx = x\arctan x - \int \dfrac{x}{1+x^{2}}dx = x\arctan x - \tfrac12\ln(1+x^{2}) \). Evaluated from \( 0 \) to \( [[b]] \) and scaled by \( [[c]] \), this gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the logarithmic correction term from \( \int x/(1+x^2)\,dx \), keeping only \( x\arctan x \).
This keeps only the boundary value \( \arctan(b) \) itself, forgetting to multiply by \( b \) or subtract the log term.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
